--- SOURCE 1: Better Life Index: A Geography of Development - Number Analytics ---
URL: https://www.numberanalytics.com/blog/better-life-index-geography-of-development

SUMMARY:
The Better Life Index is a valuable tool for understanding the complex geography of development. By examining multiple dimensions of well-being, the BLI provides a comprehensive framework for assessing development outcomes across different countries and regions .



--------------------------------------------------------------------------------


--- SOURCE 2: OECD Better Life Index ---
URL: https://www.oecd.org/en/data/tools/oecd-better-life-index.html

SUMMARY:
There is more to life than the cold numbers of GDP and economic statistics – this Index allows you to compare people's well-being across countries,



--------------------------------------------------------------------------------


--- SOURCE 3: OECD Better Life Index - Wikipedia ---
URL: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/OECD_Better_Life_Index

SUMMARY:
The index and tool were created as part of the OECD Better Life Initiative.( This initiative began in 2011 in line with the recommendations of the Commission on the Measurement of Economic Performance and Social Progress, also known as the Stiglitz-Sen-Fitoussi Commission, whose recommendations sought to address concerns that standard macroeconomic statistics like GDP failed to give a true account of people's current and future well-being.( The initiative's goals are to develop social and wellbeing indicators that can better reflect growth focusing on four key areas; environmental sustainability, increased wellbeing, falling inequality, and systems resilience. Each topic is given a score calculated from the indices used to create the topic group, you can find the raw data on the OECD Better Life Index website.( The rankings given below are calculated giving an equal weighting of 1 to each well-being topic.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Measurement of well-being across countries

![Image 4](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/98/Ambox_current_red.svg/60px-Ambox_current_red.svg.png)This article needs to be **updated**. Please help update this article to reflect recent events or newly available information._(May 2024)_

The **OECD Better Life Index**, created in May 2011 by the [Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Organisation_for_Economic_Co-operation_and_Development "Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development"), is an initiative pioneering the development of economic indicators which better capture multiple dimensions of economic and social progress.[[1]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/OECD_Better_Life_Index#cite_note-bbc_news_oecd_jersey-1)[[2]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/OECD_Better_Life_Index#cite_note-superior_quality_index-2)

The platform consists of a dashboard, that provides data and insights into key indicators - measuring areas such as wellbeing, environmental quality, quality of public services and security - alongside an interactive tool _Your Better Life Index (BLI)_,[[3]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/OECD_Better_Life_Index#cite_note-3) which encourages citizens to create their own indexes by ranking each of the indicators according to the importance in their lives.

The index and tool were created as part of the OECD Better Life Initiative.[[4]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/OECD_Better_Life_Index#cite_note-4) This initiative began in 2011 in line with the recommendations of the [Commission on the Measurement of Economic Performance and Social Progress](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Commission_on_the_Measurement_of_Economic_Performance_and_Social_Progress "Commission on the Measurement of Economic Performance and Social Progress"), also known as the Stiglitz-Sen-Fitoussi Commission, whose recommendations sought to address concerns that standard macroeconomic statistics like [GDP](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/GDP "GDP") failed to give a true account of people's current and future well-being.[[5]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/OECD_Better_Life_Index#cite_note-5) The initiative's goals are to develop social and wellbeing indicators that can better reflect growth focusing on four key areas; [environmental sustainability](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Environmental_sustainability "Environmental sustainability"), increased wellbeing, falling inequality, and systems resilience. The ['beyond growth' approach](https://www.oecd-ilibrary.org/sites/33a25ba3-en/1/2/1/index.html?itemId=/content/publication/33a25ba3-en&%20csp%20=0bf1bba564fab1eb3c5bd0ce6cd8f172&itemIGO=oecd&itemContentType=book) to economic progress is relatively new and the OECD Better Life Initiative promotes the co-production of what we might standardise by facilitating conversation between the public and policymakers.

Users can create their own economic index by ranking 11 areas of socio economic progress according to what is important to them. This generates a ranking so users can see how their country compares. Users are encouraged to share their indicators with others on the platform to view the latter's and discuss similarities and differences.

Users can also choose to share their data with the OECD and will then be asked to provide more demographic data about their situation. The OECD Better Life Initiative then analyses all users input data and reports the findings in a bi-annual report named _How's Life? Well-being_. The data used in the report consists of 80+ indicators including measures on inequality and further socio-economic indicators. The findings reflect what is important to citizens, and how their current socio-economic situations reflect in the areas of governance that they prioritise. These insights are used to encourage governments to put well-being at the centre of their policymaking by shedding light on what well-being means to their citizens.[[6]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/OECD_Better_Life_Index#cite_note-6) In this way, by using the tool, citizens can shape public policy.

Methodology and calculation
---------------------------

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=OECD_Better_Life_Index&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Methodology and calculation")]

First published on 24 May 2011, the index consists of 11 topics of well-being.[[7]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/OECD_Better_Life_Index#cite_note-7) Each of the 11 topics is made up of 1-4 indices and these are fine-tuned over time as insights are derived from data collected in previous years.[[8]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/OECD_Better_Life_Index#cite_note-8)

Initially, each of the 11 topics are equally weighted to generate scores and ranks of 30+ countries by each area of well-being. The topics are given below:

1.   Housing: [housing](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Housing "Housing") conditions and spendings (e.g. [real estate pricing](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Real_estate_pricing "Real estate pricing"))
2.   Income: [household income](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Household_income "Household income") (after taxes and transfers) and net financial wealth
3.   Jobs: [earnings](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Earnings "Earnings"), [job security](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Job_security "Job security") and [unemployment](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Unemployment "Unemployment")
4.   Community: quality of [social support](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Social_support "Social support") network
5.   Education: education and what one gets out of it
6.   Environment: quality of environment (e.g. [environmental health](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Environmental_health "Environmental health"))
7.   Governance: involvement in [democracy](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Democracy "Democracy")
8.   Health
9.   Life Satisfaction: level of [happiness](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Happiness "Happiness")
10.   Safety: murder and assault rates
11.   [Work–life balance](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Work%E2%80%93life_balance "Work–life balance")

The official definitions for the topics and the indices which make them up are stated in the [OECD Better Life Index definitions](https://www.oecd.org/statistics/OECD-Better-Life-Index-2013-definitions.pdf)

Users create their own economic indexes by scoring each of the 11 topics from 0 to 5, where 0 reflects that this topic is not important to the user and 5 it is very important. The tool shows countries ranked in a chart where each of the nations is represented by a flower, and each of the topics is a petal, where its size is defined by its score in that area. The tool was designed by Berlin-based agency [Raureif](https://raureif.net/) in collaboration with [Moritz Stefaner](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Moritz_Stefaner "Moritz Stefaner") and is very user-friendly.

The score that the user inputs for a topic is then used to calculate the weighting for that topic in the index. The weights are calculated using the formula Topic Score∑i=0 10 Topic Score{\displaystyle {\frac {\text{Topic Score}}{\sum _{i=0}^{10}{\text{Topic Score}}}}}![Image 5: {\displaystyle {\frac {\text{Topic Score}}{\sum _{i=0}^{10}{\text{Topic Score}}}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/e4ab9b96510339553126a0952c87afb73f8a7576) which ensures the sum of weights totals 100%.

Each of the 11 topics consists of between 1-4 individual measures that make up that topic. The individual measures come in many units (percent, dollar, years etc.) and so to calculate the total score for that topic, the measures are normalised, resulting in a score χ between 0 and 1 per measure. If the indicator is something negative, for example, unemployment, then we take the score as 1-χ.

For each topic, the score are then added up and divided by the total number of measures used to make up that topic, this gives the total score for the topic. For example, a countries score for health would be calculated by: Life Expectancy Score+Self-reported Health Score 2{\displaystyle {\frac {{\text{Life Expectancy Score}}+{\text{Self-reported Health Score}}}{2}}}![Image 6: {\displaystyle {\frac {{\text{Life Expectancy Score}}+{\text{Self-reported Health Score}}}{2}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/46913c2487a8cb43bdada5a7012d944730e88f4c)[[9]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/OECD_Better_Life_Index#cite_note-9)

Current rankings
----------------

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=OECD_Better_Life_Index&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Current rankings")]

### 2020

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=OECD_Better_Life_Index&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: 2020")]

The Better Life Index is not yet comparable over time as its methodology is still being fine-tuned. The OECD advises referring to the [Hows Life - Well-being database](https://stats.oecd.org/Index.aspx?DataSetCode=BLI) for a view over time.

The data shown below are the current rankings per country and topic for the year 2020. Each topic is given a score calculated from the indices used to create the topic group, you can find the raw data on the OECD Better Life Index website.[[10]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/OECD_Better_Life_Index#cite_note-10) The rankings given below are calculated giving an equal weighting of 1 to each well-being topic.

[![Image 7](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/84/Current_rankings_in_OECD_Better_Life_Index_2020.png/330px-Current_rankings_in_OECD_Better_Life_Index_2020.png)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Current_rankings_in_OECD_Better_Life_Index_2020.png)

Findings
--------

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=OECD_Better_Life_Index&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Findings")]

The fourth edition of How's Life was released in 2020; all reports can be viewed online using the [OECD Library](https://www.oecd-ilibrary.org/economics/how-s-life_23089679). Below is a summary of the most recent findings.

### General

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=OECD_Better_Life_Index&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: General")]

*   Since 2010, life expectancy has overall increased by more than one year across OECD countries
*   Over the last decade, the average homicide rate has fallen and people generally feel safer
*   Income and Jobs have on average risen over the last 10 years
*   People are generally more satisfied with their lives than they were in 2013
*   Each of the member countries is facing a very different reality however, and those countries showing improvements tend also to be those that had weaker well-being initially; they are concentrated in eastern Europe, so countries are catching up. The gap, between those improving and those that are not, has been widening, and though some countries are seeing improved well-being, this does not always come hand-in-hand with increased GDP.
*   Almost 40% of households are financially insecure
*   12% of the population across the OECD live in relative income poverty
*   Those reporting difficulties in making ends meet has almost doubled since 2010
*   There is a worrying trend of decline of time spent socialising with people spending almost half

enriched_points.expanded_content: Launched in 2011, this index evaluates well-being across countries through 11 dimensions, promoting a comprehensive understanding beyond GDP, including health, education, and life satisfaction.
The HDI measures a country's average achievements in health, education, and income, emphasizing human well-being and development as pivotal for national progress.
The GPI accounts for economic, social, and environmental factors, aiming to provide a more accurate reflection of a nation's true progress by incorporating well-being and sustainability.
